title: Cloudflare DDoS Mitigation Event Volume by Attack Vector (`cloudflare:json`)
dataSources: Cloudflare Logpush JSON delivered to Splunk HEC/S3 with `sourcetype=cloudflare:json` (HTTP requests, firewall, Workers, DNS, Access as configured)
app: Cloudflare App for Splunk (Splunkbase 4501) + Logpush JSON (`cloudflare:json`)
spl:
index=waf sourcetype="cloudflare:json" earliest=-24h
| where match(_raw,"(?i)ddos|mitigat|dos")
| spath MitigationReason
| spath RuleID
| stats count by MitigationReason, EdgeColoCode
| sort -count
description: L3/4 versus L7 attacks need different runbooks. Logpush JSON ties mitigation reasons to colo and rule context.
value: Security and NOC teams gain real-time visibility into active DDoS attacks, understand whether mitigated traffic is L3/L4 volumetric or L7 application-layer, and document attack patterns for regulatory reporting and DDoS protection investment decisions.
implementation: Configure Logpush jobs per dataset; route all into a unified `cloudflare:json` sourcetype or use transforms to normalize. Use `spath` for PascalCase fields. Create macros per zone name.
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Web.Web
  by Web.user, Web.url, Web.http_method span=1h
| sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Anomalies from new SaaS app rollouts, browser updates, or vendor URL category changes.
grandmaExplanation: We track when someone tries to flood our website with fake traffic to knock it offline, and we see how well our protection held up and what kind of attack it was.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Cloudflare App for Splunk (Splunkbase 4501) v2.x on the Search Head. Logpush configured for the **Firewall Events** dataset (for L7 DDoS) and optionally the **Network Analytics Logs** dataset (for L3/L4 DDoS, Enterprise only).
• L7 DDoS events appear in Firewall Events with `Source` values like `l7ddos` or `Action=ddos_dynamic`. L3/L4 DDoS events require the Network Analytics dataset with fields: `AttackType`, `MitigationType`, `DestinationPort`, `SourcePort`, `Protocol`.
• Data routed to `index=waf` (firewall events) and optionally `index=netflow` (network analytics) with `sourcetype=cloudflare:json`.
• All Cloudflare plans include basic DDoS protection. Advanced DDoS Analytics and Network Analytics Logs require Enterprise.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Verify L7 DDoS events:
```spl
index=waf sourcetype="cloudflare:json" earliest=-7d
| spath Source | spath Action
| where Source="l7ddos" OR match(Action, "(?i)ddos")
| stats count by Source, Action
```

For L3/L4 (Enterprise only):
```spl
index=netflow sourcetype="cloudflare:json" earliest=-7d
| spath AttackType | spath MitigationType
| where isnotnull(AttackType)
| stats count by AttackType, MitigationType
```

DDoS events are inherently bursty — you may see zero for days, then thousands in minutes during an attack. No events in quiet periods is normal and healthy.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

Primary search — DDoS mitigation events by vector:
```spl
index=waf sourcetype="cloudflare:json" earliest=-24h
| spath Source | spath Action | spath ClientRequestHost | spath RuleID
| where Source="l7ddos" OR match(Action, "(?i)ddos")
| eval attack_vector=coalesce(RuleID, Source, "unknown")
| stats count as mitigated_requests dc(ClientIP) as attack_sources earliest(_time) as start latest(_time) as end by attack_vector, ClientRequestHost
| eval duration_min=round((end - start)/60, 0)
| eval intensity=round(mitigated_requests/max(duration_min, 1), 0)
| sort - mitigated_requests
```

Understanding this SPL: Groups DDoS mitigation events by attack vector (identified by rule or source classification) and target zone. The `intensity` field (requests per minute) distinguishes between low-and-slow attacks and volumetric floods. High `attack_sources` with moderate `intensity` suggests a botnet; low `attack_sources` with extreme `intensity` suggests a single powerful source or amplification attack.

Attack timeline:
```spl
index=waf sourcetype="cloudflare:json" earliest=-48h
| spath Source | where Source="l7ddos" OR match(Action, "(?i)ddos")
| spath ClientRequestHost
| timechart span=5m count by ClientRequestHost
```

Step 3 — Validate
(a) In Cloudflare: Security → DDoS → check attack timeline and mitigation summaries. Compare attack start/end times and volume with Splunk.
(b) During or after an attack, check the Cloudflare email notification (sent automatically for significant attacks) and compare the stated attack vector and volume with Splunk data.
(c) Verify L3/L4 coverage (Enterprise): Cloudflare → Analytics → Network Analytics. If this dashboard shows attacks not visible in Splunk, the Network Analytics Logpush dataset is not configured.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard ("Cloudflare — DDoS Protection"):
• Row 1 — Single-value tiles: "Active attacks", "Requests mitigated (24h)", "Attack sources".
• Row 2 — Timeline: DDoS events over 48h (5-minute bins) showing attack start, peak, and end.
• Row 3 — Table: attack vector, target zone, duration, intensity, source count.
• Row 4 — Pie chart: attack vector distribution over 30 days for pattern analysis.

Alert: Trigger immediately when DDoS mitigation count exceeds 1000 in any 5-minute window. Critical severity — the site is under active attack. Even though Cloudflare mitigates automatically, the NOC needs awareness for communication and origin protection.

Runbook:
1. **L7 DDoS on a single zone** — Cloudflare mitigates automatically. Monitor origin health (UC-17.4.6) — if origin is struggling despite mitigation, enable "Under Attack Mode" in the Cloudflare dashboard for the affected zone.
2. **L3/L4 volumetric attack** — Cloudflare absorbs at the edge. Check if your origin's upstream bandwidth is saturated (possible if traffic leaks around Cloudflare). Verify all origin traffic is proxied through Cloudflare.
3. **Recurring attacks at the same time daily** — Pattern suggests a targeted campaign. Document for legal/law enforcement and consider Cloudflare's DDoS investigation team (Enterprise).

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **No DDoS events ever** — This is good (no attacks). Do not create synthetic test data. Verify the Logpush job is running by checking for other firewall events (`Source=firewallManaged`).
• **DDoS events in Cloudflare dashboard but not in Splunk** — L3/L4 attacks are only in the Network Analytics Logs dataset, not Firewall Events. Configure a separate Logpush job for Network Analytics (Enterprise only).
• **Very high volume during attack overwhelms Splunk HEC** — Cloudflare can generate millions of events/minute during a large attack. Consider Logpush sampling or routing DDoS events to a separate high-volume index with shorter retention.
